perturbation.original_text: This Collaboration Agreement (the "Agreement") is made as of April 14th, 2020 (the "Effective Date") by and between Anixa Biosciences, Inc., a Delaware corporation, located at 3150 Almaden Expressway, Suite 250, San Jose, CA 95118, U.S.A. ("Anixa"), and OntoChem GmbH, a German limited liability company, located at Blücherstr. 24, D-06120 Halle (Saale), Germany ("OntoChem").
perturbation.changed_text: This Collaboration Agreement (the "Agreement") is made as of April 14th, 2019 (the "Effective Date") by and between Anixa Biosciences, Inc., a Delaware corporation, located at 3150 Almaden Expressway, Suite 250, San Jose, CA 95118, U.S.A. ("Anixa"), and OntoChem GmbH, a German limited liability company, located at Blücherstr. 24, D-06120 Halle (Saale), Germany ("OntoChem").
perturbation.explanation: Changing the effective date introduces a discrepancy, as the document is dated June 9, 2020, and refers to the effective date of April 14, 2020. Changing the effective date to April 14, 2019 will make the contract suspect from the start.
perturbation.law_explanation: Changing the date to 2019 creates an inconsistency. The agreement itself is dated June 9, 2020, and references 2020 elsewhere, causing an initial factual error. This alteration undermines the agreement's validity because fundamental contract law requires clear and consistent agreement on essential terms like the effective date. Altering it to a prior year makes the agreement's internal consistency questionable from the outset.

perturbation.justification: The modified text changes the effective date of the contract from April 14, 2020, to April 14, 2019, while the document itself is dated June 9, 2020, creating an inconsistency. According to the Law Explanation, this undermines the agreement's validity because contract law requires clear agreement on essential terms like the effective date. The Perturbation Explanation further states that changing the effective date will make the contract suspect from the start. The Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction definition applies here because the time-sensitive obligation (the effective date) does not align with the date of the document itself, making the contractual terms questionable.

perturbation.original_text: OntoChem will provide Anixa with weekly (or more frequently as requested) updates regarding its progress under the Research Program via teleconference, videoconference or e-mail, and the Parties will make appropriate personnel available in a timely manner to discuss and provide feedback in regard to such updates.
perturbation.changed_text: OntoChem will provide Anixa with updates regarding its progress under the Research Program every six months via teleconference, videoconference or e-mail, and the Parties will make appropriate personnel available in a timely manner to discuss and provide feedback in regard to such updates.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the frequency of updates from weekly to every six months could be problematic if the research involves human subjects and requires regular reporting to an Institutional Review Board (IRB).
perturbation.law_explanation: If the research involves human subjects and is subject to IRB oversight, changing the update frequency to every six months may violate the requirement for continuing IRB review and prompt reporting of unanticipated problems. 45 CFR § 46.109(e) mandates that IRBs conduct continuing review of research at intervals appropriate to the degree of risk, but not less than once per year. Moreover, it is standard practice for IRBs to expect more frequent updates for higher-risk studies. Infrequent updates may prevent the IRB from fulfilling its duty to protect human subjects, particularly if unforeseen issues arise during the research.

Navigate to: 45 CFR 46 The HHS regulations for the protection of human subjects in research at 45 CFR 46 include five subparts. Subpart A, also known as the Common Rule, provides a robust set of protections for research subjects. The Common Rule, subpart A, was revised in recent years (the “2018 Requirements”). Subparts B, C, and D provide additional protections for certain populations in research. Subpart E provides requirements for institutional review board registration. Access the regulatory language using the links below. 2018 Requirements - official e-Code of Federal Regulation Exemptions - official e-Code of Federal Regulation Subpart B Additional protections for research with pregnant women and fetuses Subpart C Additional protections for research with prisoners Subpart D Additional protections for research with children Subpart E Requirements for IRB registration List of Expedited Categories (1998) In general, research initiated before January 21, 2019 continues to comply with the pre-2018 Common Rule unless the institution chose to transition it to the revised Common Rule. Pre-2018 Common Rule Content created by Office for Human Research Protections (OHRP) Content last reviewed February 11, 2025
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the frequency of updates from weekly to every six months. The law explanation states that this may violate the requirement for continuing IRB review and prompt reporting of unanticipated problems, referencing 45 CFR § 46.109(e), which mandates continuing review at intervals appropriate to the degree of risk, but not less than once per year. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references 45 CFR 46, indicating this regulation concerns the protection of human subjects. The change directly affects the frequency of updates, potentially hindering the IRB's ability to adequately oversee research involving human subjects as described in the law explanation.

perturbation.original_text: Payments to OntoChem will be made by check or by wire transfer of immediately available funds to such bank account as designated in writing by OntoChem from time to time. Taxes (and any penalties and interest thereon) imposed on any payment made by Anixa to OntoChem will be the responsibility of OntoChem. The fees for the respective bank transfers will be borne by Anixa.
perturbation.changed_text: Payments to OntoChem will be made at Anixa's discretion. Taxes (and any penalties and interest thereon) imposed on any payment made by Anixa to OntoChem will be the responsibility of OntoChem. The fees for the respective bank transfers will be borne by Anixa.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the payment method and timeline and replacing it with 'Anixa's discretion' renders the payment terms too vague and unenforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: By stating that payments will be made at Anixa's discretion, the agreement fails to provide a definite term for payment, which is essential for contract enforceability under UCC § 2-204. The UCC requires that a contract have reasonably certain terms, including the time and manner of payment. Leaving the payment schedule entirely to one party's discretion renders the payment obligation too indefinite to be legally binding, creating a legally suspect agreement.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-204. Formation in General. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-204. Formation in General. (1) A contract for sale of goods may be made in any manner sufficient to show agreement , including conduct by both parties which recognizes the existence of such a contract . (2) An agreement sufficient to constitute a contract for sale may be found even though the moment of its making is undetermined. (3) Even though one or more terms are left open a contract for sale does not fail for indefiniteness if the parties have intended to make a contract and there is a reasonably certain basis for giving an appropriate remedy. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: rship that put all the component commercial laws together in a comprehensive Uniform Commercial Code that was offered to the states for their consideration in 1951. Pennsylvania became the first state to adopt the UCC in 1953, and every other state followed suit over the next twenty years. News ULC Statement on Ownership of Investment Property under Uniform Commercial Code Article 8 ULC Statement on Central Bank Digital Currencies and the Uniform Commercial Code Cooperation with ALI The UCC is a joint project of the Uniform Law Commission and the American Law Institute. Recognizing that drafting a combined commercial code was a massive undertaking, the ULC invited ALI to participate in the codification project, and the ALI board accepted the invitation in 1942. Over the next ten years the two organizations collaborated at drafting meetings funded in large part by a generous grant from the Maurice and Laura Falk Foundation, with additional funding contributed from law firms, banks, and businesses that recognized the need for uniform commercial laws. The UCC Today The UCC is maintained under the guidance of the Permanent Editorial Board for the Uniform Commercial Code (PEB) , comprised of members appointed by the ULC and the ALI. The PEB, established in 1961, monitors developments in commercial law, recommends UCC amendments and revisions when necessary, and publishes official commentary to help courts interpret specific UCC provisions. An endowment established with the original Falk Foundation grant funding and replenished with UCC publishing royalties is available to fund UCC drafting projects. Articles Article 1, General Provisions Uniform Commercial Code Article 1 contains definitions and general provisions applicable as default rules to transactions covered under other articles of the UCC. Article 1 was last revised in 2001, with a few minor amendments since then to harmonize with recent revisions of other UCC articles. View Article 1, General Provisions Article 2, Sales Uniform Commercial Code Article 2 governs the sale of goods. It was part of the original Uniform Commercial Code approved in 1951. Article 2 represented a revision and modernization of the Uniform Sales Act, which was originally approved by the National Conference of Commissioners on Uniform State Laws in 1906. The Uniform Law Commission and American Law Institute approved a revised Article 2 in 2003 that was not adopted in any state, and was subsequently withdrawn by both organizations in 2011. Thus the 1951 version of Article 2 is the most recent official version. View Article 2, Sales Article 2A, Leases Uniform Commercial Code Article 2A governs leases of personal property. It was first added to the Uniform Commercial Code in 1987 and amended in 1990. A revision was approved by the Uniform Law Commission and the American Law Institute in 2003, but was not adopted in any jurisdiction and subsequently withdrawn by both organizations in 2011. Thus, the 1987 version of Article 2A, as amended in 1990, remains the official text. View Article 2A, Leases Article 3, Negotiable Instruments Uniform Commercial Code Article 3 governs negotiable instruments: drafts (including checks) and notes representing a promise to pay a sum of money, and that have independent value because they are negotiable. An instrument is negotiable if it can be transferred to another person and remain enforceable against the person who originally made the promise to pay. The substance of Article 3 has its roots in the Negotiable Instrument Law first approved by the National Conference of Commissioners on Uniform State Laws in 1896. That early uniform law was revised and incorporated into the original version of the UCC in 1951, and a further revision was approved in 1990. Finally, a set of amendments to UCC Articles 3 and 4 was approved in 2002. View Article 3, Negotiable Instruments Article 4, Bank Deposits and Collections Uniform Commercial Code Article 4 governs bank deposits and collecti
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces specific payment terms with 'Anixa's discretion,' creating a legally suspect agreement. This contradicts the UCC requirement for reasonably certain terms, including payment time and manner. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing UCC § 2-204, states that a contract for the sale of goods can be made in any manner showing agreement. However, it also implies the necessity of 'a reasonably certain basis for giving an appropriate remedy,' which the altered text undermines by granting payment discretion to one party. The modification removes the original contract's defined payment method, rendering the payment obligation too indefinite to be legally binding, as highlighted in the Law Explanation.
